lesson.title: Functions I: why, define, and call
masteryOutcomes: Understand how functions reduce repetition and improve reuse.
Understand the structure of Python functions.
Define a function with one or two parameters and call it with literals and variables.

concept: Why functions exist, and how to define one with def and run it by calling it with arguments
prerequisites: Creating and using variables to store values
Printing values with print(), including multiple values with commas

explanations.text: Imagine you teach a friend a little handshake once and give it a name. From then on you just say "do the greeting handshake" — no re-explaining every step. A function is exactly that: you wrap a few lines of code under a name with def, and later you run them any time by calling the name with parentheses. The parentheses can even carry a value in — greet("Ada") — so the same steps work for Ada, for Grace, for anyone.

The moment you catch yourself copying the same lines with tiny changes, you want a function. def greet(name): starts one — the word def, a name you choose, parentheses holding a parameter, and a colon. The indented lines underneath are the body: the steps that run. Defining runs nothing; the body runs only when you call the function, like greet("Ada") or greet(friend) with a variable. Whatever value sits in the call slides into the parameter name. Two parameters work the same way, separated by a comma: def add(a, b): called as add(3, 4).

Mechanically, def creates a function and binds it to a name — the body is stored, not executed. Execution happens at the call: Python matches each argument to a parameter by position (first value to first parameter) and runs the body with those values filled in. The caller can pass a literal like "Ada" or a variable like friend — the function only ever sees the value, never the caller's variable name. Get the count wrong and Python is precise about it: greet() gives TypeError: greet() missing 1 required positional argument: 'name', while greet("Ada", "Bob") gives TypeError: greet() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given. One more subtlety: print(greet) without parentheses shows something like <function greet at 0x101c2f380> — the parentheses are what actually run it.

examples:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello,", name)

greet("Ada")
greet("Grace")

def greet(name):
    print("Hello,", name)

friend = "Sam"
greet(friend)

def cheer():
    print("Go team!")

cheer()
cheer()
cheer()

hintLadder.text: Start with the shape of the def line: def, the function's name, parentheses, then a colon at the end.
Every line that belongs to the function must be indented underneath the def line — that indentation is what makes it the body.
A parameter is just a name inside the parentheses — pick one that describes the value the caller will send in.
Defining runs nothing. Check: does your code actually call the function afterwards — its name, parentheses, and a value inside them?

misconceptions.trigger: Defines the function but never calls it, then wonders why nothing prints
misconceptions.correction: def only teaches Python the recipe — nothing runs until you call it. Add greet("Ada") below the definition, at the left margin, and run again.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes the function name without parentheses, e.g. print(greet)
misconceptions.correction: That shows something like <function greet at 0x101c2f380> instead of running anything. Parentheses are the go button: greet("Ada") runs the body.

misconceptions.trigger: Leaves the colon off the def line, e.g. def greet(name)
misconceptions.correction: Python stops with SyntaxError: expected ':'. Every def line ends with a colon — it announces that an indented body comes next.

misconceptions.trigger: Writes the body at the left margin instead of indenting it
misconceptions.correction: Python raises IndentationError: expected an indented block after function definition on line 1. Indent the body (four spaces is the convention) so Python knows those lines belong to the function.

misconceptions.trigger: Calls greet() with empty parentheses when greet has a parameter
misconceptions.correction: Python answers TypeError: greet() missing 1 required positional argument: 'name' — the call must supply one value, like greet("Ada"). A parameter is a promise the caller has to keep.

misconceptions.trigger: Thinks the variable passed in must have the same name as the parameter, e.g. only a variable called name may go into greet(name)
misconceptions.correction: Any variable or literal works — only the value travels. friend = "Sam" then greet(friend) prints Hello, Sam. The parameter name lives inside the function; the caller's names are its own business.

misconceptions.trigger: Confidently claims functions are pointless because copy-paste does the same job
misconceptions.correction: Copy-paste means every future fix must be repeated in every copy. With a function you fix one place. Change the greeting text inside greet once, run it, and watch every call update at the same time.

checks.prompt: Define a function greet that takes one parameter name and prints Hello, <name>! — then call it twice: once with a literal like "Ada", and once passing a variable.
acceptanceCriteria: Uses def greet(name): with the colon, and an indented body
The body prints a greeting that uses the parameter
Calls the function twice: once with a string literal, once with a variable
Runs without errors and prints two greetings on two lines
checks.passFeedback: That's the whole cycle — define once, call twice, and the same body served two different people.
checks.failFeedback: Walk the shape: a def line ending in a colon, an indented body that uses name, then two unindented calls below — one with a quoted string, one with a variable you created.

checks.prompt:
Predict before running: exactly what does this program print, line by line?
def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)

add(2, 3)
add(10, 20)
acceptanceCriteria: Predicts 5 as the first line of output
Explains that the body runs once per call, with each call's values sliding into a and b
checks.passFeedback: Exactly — one body, two calls, two different results. That's reuse in action.
checks.failFeedback: Trace one call at a time: add(2, 3) puts 2 into a and 3 into b, so the body prints 5. Then the second call starts fresh with 10 and 20.

practice.prompt: Write a function ticket(name, seat) that prints one line like: Ada -> seat 12 (the name, an arrow, the word seat, and the seat number). Call it for two different people, and at least one call must pass variables instead of literals.
acceptanceCriteria: Defines ticket with two parameters and an indented body
The body uses both parameters in a single print
Calls the function twice with different values, at least once with variables
Runs without errors and prints two ticket lines

practice.prompt: In your own words: why is defining a function better than copying the same three lines five times? Give two reasons.
rubric: One place to change: editing the definition updates every call, while copies must each be fixed separately
Reuse with different data: the same body works for any argument passed in
Bonus: a good function name documents what the code does

practice.prompt:
What does this program print?
def greet(name):
    print("Hi,", name)

greet
options: Nothing — greet is never actually called, because there are no parentheses
TypeError: greet() missing 1 required positional argument: 'name'
practice.explanation: A bare greet without parentheses just mentions the function — it never runs the body, so nothing prints. Parentheses trigger the call: greet() would raise the missing-argument TypeError, and greet("Ada") would print Hi, Ada.

pedagogyRules: Teach the anatomy one piece at a time in this order — def line, indented body, then the call — and have the student run code after each piece, predicting the output first.
If the student asks a jump-ahead question (return values, default parameters, loops), answer in one sentence, note that return arrives in Functions III, and come back to define-and-call.

If the student says they already know functions, skip the explanation: ask them to predict print(greet) without parentheses and greet("Ada", "Bob") when greet takes one parameter; two correct predictions with reasons means go straight to the check.
If the student is lost, shrink to zero parameters: def cheer(): with one print inside, called three times. Only add a parameter once that runs and makes sense.
When the student is wrong but confident, don't argue — have them run their exact line and read the real output or traceback aloud, then name the one rule it demonstrates.
If the student wants to skip ahead, allow it once they pass the greet check on the first try; otherwise fix one thing first.

If a TypeError or IndentationError causes frustration, acknowledge it, then point out that the last line of the traceback names the exact problem — count the arguments or check the indentation, one fix.
If asked why this matters, answer in one line — every app they use is thousands of functions calling each other; this is the unit real programs are built from — then continue the lesson.

failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Read the last traceback line literally. missing 1 required positional argument: 'name' means the call supplied too few values; takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given means too many. Point at the call site, not the def line, and never invent a different cause.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Tutor validates a program where the function is defined but never called
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Only confirm success when output actually appears. A definition alone prints nothing — if the student's run shows no output, say so and ask where the call is.

failurePlaybooks.failureMode: Scaffolding gap — tutor dumps the complete def plus call
failurePlaybooks.correctMove: Give one hint from the ladder at a time and let the student run code between hints; the student types every line of the definition and the call themselves.
During checks and practice, never type the working def line or the call — describe the shape (def, name, parentheses, colon, indented body) and let the student produce the code.

chunks.text: A function wraps a few lines of code under a name so they can be reused. Why it matters: with copy-paste, every fix must be repeated in every copy; with a function, you fix the one definition and every call updates. Defining uses the keyword def; running the function later is called calling it.

The anatomy of a definition: def, the function's name, parentheses holding zero or more parameters, and a colon — then an indented body underneath. Worked example:
def greet(name):
    print("Hello,", name)
Defining runs nothing: Python just stores the recipe under the name greet.

Calling runs the body: write the function's name with parentheses and the values to send in. greet("Ada") prints Hello, Ada. The value in the call is the argument; it slides into the parameter name. Literals and variables both work — friend = "Sam" then greet(friend) prints Hello, Sam, because only the value travels, never the variable's name.

Two parameters are separated by a comma and filled by position — the first argument goes to the first parameter. Worked example:
def add(a, b):
    print(a + b)
add(3, 4) prints 7, because a becomes 3 and b becomes 4. The same body serves every pair of numbers you call it with.

Common stumbles, with Python's exact words: leaving off the colon gives SyntaxError: expected ':'; an unindented body gives IndentationError: expected an indented block after function definition on line 1; greet() with a missing value gives TypeError: greet() missing 1 required positional argument: 'name'; greet("Ada", "Bob") gives TypeError: greet() takes 1 positional argument but 2 were given; and a bare greet without parentheses runs nothing at all.

celebration.headline: You just built your first reusable machine.
celebration.recap: You saw why functions kill repetition, learned the def-name-parentheses-colon-body shape, and defined functions with one and two parameters — then called them with literals and variables and watched one body serve many values.
celebration.unlocked: Next: Functions II — we go inside the function while it runs and trace exactly how values travel from the call into the parameters.

summaryBullets: You can now understand how functions reduce repetition and improve reuse.
You can now understand the structure of Python functions.
You can now define a function with one or two parameters and call it with literals and variables.
